perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall not be entitled to receive a share of any revenues and shall have no claim to any revenues earned and collected by Fox, or a third-party Fox designates, for the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products which Fox distributes through its and its assigns' direct-to-consumer distribution channels.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall not be entitled to receive a share of any revenues and shall have no claim to any revenues earned and collected by Fox, or a third-party Fox designates, for the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products.
perturbation.explanation: This clause removes the specification that this revenue exclusion only applies to direct-to-consumer distribution channels. By omitting this qualifier, it could be interpreted as Licensee having no claim to revenues from *any* distribution channel, potentially contradicting state franchise laws if the arrangement meets the definition of a franchise agreement and the licensee is not properly compensated.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Franchise Relations Act governs franchise agreements. If the licensee (Glu Mobile) is essentially operating as a franchisee by distributing Fox's products, then the omission of the 'direct-to-consumer' clause could lead to a violation of franchise laws if the agreement fails to provide adequate compensation or other protections required under the act.

SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 5.5. Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 1. Definitions [20000 - 20010] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) 20000. This chapter shall be known and may be referred to as the California Franchise Relations Act. (Added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. Effective October 1, 1980. Operative January 1, 1981, by Sec. 6 of Ch. 1355.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'direct-to-consumer distribution channels' from the original text broadens the scope of revenue exclusion for the Licensee. This could create a situation where the licensee is not entitled to revenues from distribution channels beyond direct-to-consumer, potentially violating California's Franchise Relations Act (as mentioned in the Law Explanation) if the agreement qualifies as a franchise. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies BPC 20000 as the California Franchise Relations Act, which suggests the change, by potentially denying revenues, could contradict the protections offered under this Act if a franchise relationship exists.

perturbation.original_text: Except as herein expressly amended or by necessary implication modified by this Amendment, the Agreement in all other respects is hereby ratified and shall continue in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as herein expressly amended or by necessary implication modified by this Amendment, the Agreement in all other respects is hereby ratified and shall continue in full force and effect. The parties agree that any disputes arising from this agreement will be settled through binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'The parties agree that any disputes arising from this agreement will be settled through binding arbitration' without explicitly providing an opt-out clause or meeting other requirements for valid arbitration agreements under California law, there's a risk of creating an unenforceable or illegal arbitration agreement, especially if it affects consumer rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1295
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1295 requires specific language and disclosures for arbitration clauses in contracts, particularly those involving medical services. While this agreement isn't for medical services, the principle of requiring clear and conspicuous arbitration agreements applies broadly. Adding a simple 'binding arbitration' clause without these safeguards could render it unenforceable under California law, especially if it's deemed to unfairly limit consumer rights.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9.1. ARBITRATION OF MEDICAL MALPRACTICE [1295- 1295.] ( Title 9.1 added by Stats. 1975, 2nd Ex. Sess., Ch. 1. ) 1295. (a) Any contract for medical services which contains a provision for arbitration of any dispute as to professional negligence of a health care provider shall have such provision as the first article of the contract and shall be expressed in the following language: “It is understood that any dispute as to medical malpractice, that is as to whether any medical services rendered under this contract were unnecessary or unauthorized or were improperly, negligently or incompetently rendered, will be determined by submission to arbitration as provided by California law, and not by a lawsuit or resort to court process except as California law provides for judicial review of arbitration proceedings. Both parties to this contract, by entering into it, are giving up their constitutional right to have any such dispute decided in a court of law before a jury, and instead are accepting the use of arbitration.” (b) Immediately before the signature line provided for the individual contracting for the medical services must appear the followin
perturbation.justification: The added arbitration clause, according to the Perturbation and Law Explanations, may not meet the requirements for valid arbitration agreements under California law, especially concerning consumer rights. Snippet 1, CCP 1295, specifies language requirements for arbitration clauses in medical service contracts, indicating a need for clear and conspicuous arbitration agreements. While not directly applicable, it suggests a broader principle. The 'binding arbitration' clause, if it unfairly limits consumer rights and lacks necessary disclosures, risks being unenforceable, creating a contradiction.
